# Transcrição: 07-ACTIVITY- Creating Surprise in Your Personal Story

- **Fonte:** 07-ACTIVITY- Creating Surprise in Your Personal Story
- **Duração:** 04:23
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

Now it's time for you to tackle surprise in your personal story. Don't tell me there's no surprise. I don't want to hear it because it's not true. Every story has at least one surprise because, remember, stories are about change, transformation or realization, and those moments are always surprising to us. It is shocking to us when we suddenly see the world in a new way, when we suddenly view ourselves in a new way, when we see our parents or our children in a new light, when we see ourselves as different people. That's always surprising, which means the end of every story is going to somehow be a little surprising. There may be other surprises along the way, but don't tell me you don't have a surprise in your story because the last moments of your story will at least be surprising. So here's what you're going to do. I know I scolded you, I'm sorry, but I hear it a lot. Here's what you're going to do. Take a look at your story. Just like you mapped out the emotional moves in your story, just like you identified how you felt through each moment in the story, I want you to find moments of surprise, small and big, little moments where, oh wow, I can't believe that happened or wow, that was weird or that was strange, and then big profound moments like Maddie the dog, the bride married the best man. I can't believe it. Find the moments of surprise in the story and identify them. And then ask yourself, in order for this surprise to make sense to my audience, what do they need to know? When I said water over raisin brand, that was surprising. It didn't require any effort on my part because you know as a human being that milk goes over raisin brand, not water. That's why it's surprising. That surprise didn't require any effort from me because you already had the knowledge that I needed you to have. I didn't have to explain how raisin brand and milk work. So therefore it was surprising. You'll have those moments in your story. Those are wonderful moments. You don't have to do any heavy lifting. You basically just have to say it in a clever way. Water poured over raisin brand is the best way to say that. It's the most surprising way. Other surprises, as you now know, require some work ahead of time. Identify the surprise and then ask yourself, what does my audience need to know that they already know, like how raisin brand and milk work? But what does my audience need to know that I have to teach them? And then how will I teach them that information? Make the list of the things they need to know. For me, it was they need to know that there was a dog that was once named after me. They need to know that the couple who named the dog after me were married and that I was present at their wedding. I was their DJ, the funnest guy they knew. All of those things were things my audience needed to know for the surprise to pay off. You do the same and then decide where you're going to put that information.

## [03:04 - 04:23]

You're going to conceal it in a list perhaps or make it funny. You're going to give it another reason to exist within the story. You're also going to place that information as far away from the surprise as possible. You don't want the thing the audience needs to know to happen right before the surprise. That is very unsatisfying. You need to separate it. You need to make it part of the landscape, part of the culture of your story, part of the world building that you're doing in your story. You need to let that information sit with them for a while so they can kind of forget about it. That's importance so that it can come back in surprise. You're going to do all of those things now. See if you can find a way to teach your audience what they need to know so that you can surprise them, and then find those little moments of surprise along the way, and find clever ways to say them. The phrasing of a surprise is always critical. There's no shower curtain in my bathroom, so when I take a shower, the whole bathroom takes a shower. Those words are chosen very carefully to maximize the surprise. Find those little moments that don't require any heavy lifting, and find ways to say them that you think are the most surprising. All of that work, I want you to go do now.
